stories.title: Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026: Navigating Capital Flow & Deal Dynamics
stories.excerpt: The multifamily sector is seeing investment competition return, as sellers adjust pricing expectations and buyers show steadier interest. Our expert panel on navigating capital flow and deal dynamics at Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026 discussed strategies for securing financing and managing loan maturities. Industry leaders shared their acquisition strategies for underwriting deals, identifying distress and acquiring assets. “There have been a lot of changes in the agency space, and the small balance program has really started to shrink with a bigger focus across the board for larger assets,” said Igor Zhizhin, Director at Lument. “Large deals are getting incredibly competitive capital.” The panel covered various approaches to challenges in the multifamily market. “One of the things that we’ve tried to do to try to solve some of these issues is to go up pretty high up on the leverage level, up to 85 or 90% of loan to cost, if we have a quick value creation component and we can get paid back relatively quickly,” said Stratos Athanassiades, Regional Manager, for Red Oak Capital Holdings. “So if the business plan works, we can provide some of that equity at rates that are a lot lower than they’d be paying.” The post Connect Midwest Multifamily Trends 2026: Navigating Capital Flow & Deal Dynamics appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Agency small-balance multifamily loans shrinking; lenders favoring larger assets with competitive capital.
Developers using 85-90% loan-to-cost leverage with quick value-creation plans to lower equity costs.
Seller pricing expectations adjusting downward; buyer interest steadying across Midwest multifamily market.
stories.summary: Midwest multifamily capital markets are rebalancing after volatility: agency lending in smaller deals is contracting while larger assets attract intense competition and better terms. Developers are responding by structuring higher-leverage deals (85-90% LTC) with near-term value creation to reduce equity requirements and lower total cost of capital. Sellers are moderating price expectations and finding renewed buyer interest, signaling a stabilization phase rather than distress liquidation.
stories.impact: This signals a bifurcated capital market favoring scale—smaller Midwest acquisitions will face tighter, more expensive financing, while large-asset developers and portfolio players with quick hold periods can access competitive, high-leverage terms. Developers should prioritize deal size and value-add timeline when underwriting in the Midwest; marginal projects may no longer pencil at bank terms.

stories.title: Wells Fargo Provides $46M Refi for SoCal Shopping Center
stories.excerpt: A Target-anchored shopping center in Southern California’s San Gabriel Valley has secured refinancing, Walker & Dunlop announced Thursday. Wells Fargo provided $46 million in fixed-rate, interest-only permanent financing for the 336,718-square-foot Rosemead Place in Rosemead, Calif., in Los Angeles County. PropertyShark lists MPM Partners, run by Herbert Yang, as the property’s owner. The shopping center is about 10 miles east of Downtown L.A. at 3506-3864 Rosemead Boulevard, at the intersection with Interstate 10. In addition to Target, the tenants at Rosemead Place include LA Fitness, Ross Dress for Less, PetSmart, ULTA Beauty and Dollar Tree. Trevor Fase and Lynn Pearson led the Walker & Dunlop team, while Joel Minugh and James Ko represented Wells Fargo in-house. “Well-located, anchored retail assets like Rosemead Place continue to be viewed extremely favorably by a wide range of capital providers, including insurance companies, commercial mortgage-backed security lenders, and regional and national banks,” Fase said in a statement. “Despite several moving parts, including near-term lease rollover, we successfully closed the transaction in less than 60 days.” The San Gabriel Valley’s retail real estate market is putting pressure on landlords with declining asking rents, according to Lee & Associates’ first-quarter analysis. The market’s average triple-net asking rate dropped slightly for at least the previous four quarters to about $2.81 per square foot per month. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Wells Fargo refinances $46M for 336K-SF Target-anchored shopping center in San Gabriel Valley, Los Angeles County.
Rosemead Place near I-10: fixed-rate, interest-only permanent financing; lender confirms strong appetite for well-anchored retail.
Mixed-use and retail capital still flowing to best-located, credit-tenanted assets despite broader CRE headwinds.
stories.summary: Wells Fargo provided $46 million in fixed-rate, permanent financing for Rosemead Place, a 336,718-square-foot Target-anchored shopping center in the San Gabriel Valley east of Downtown LA. The deal confirms lender appetite for well-positioned, credit-anchored retail despite macro uncertainty. Multiple major tenants (LA Fitness, Ross, ULTA, Dollar Tree) support stable cash flow.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Signals that institutional capital—banks, insurers, CMBS lenders—remains available for prime retail, but this capital is NOT migrating to multifamily; it reinforces that best-location, strongest-credit assets across all property types still command financing, while secondary properties face capital constraints.

stories.title: Chicago/Midwest People & Company News, week of June 26, 2026
stories.excerpt: Cresa announced that Julie Northcutt-Dunn joined as president, markets, a newly created executive role focused on driving growth across Cresa’s market operations through strategic planning, operational excellence and organic growth initiatives. She will be based in Cresa’s Chicago headquarters. Northcutt-Dunn joins from Colliers, where she was recently executive managing director, overseeing Chicago, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Omaha, St. Louis and Nashville. The Inland Real Estate Group has appointed Jacqueline Dagan as associate general counsel and Dhaval Parikh as head of institutional capital formation, adding two seasoned leaders to its institutional asset management platform, Inland Institutional. Dagan brings more than two decades of legal experience across investment management, real estate funds and alternative assets. Parikh brings nearly 20 years of experience in capital raising, investor relations, and product development within the real estate investment industry. Buffalo Grove-based PREMIER Design + Build Group announced the promotion of Stacy Reid to vice president of its new Reno office. The appointment marks a significant step in PREMIER’s continued growth across the United States. Reid has successfully delivered complex projects throughout the Western United States, including adaptive reuse, tenant improvements, healthcare, office, and large-scale industrial developments. JLL has appointed Chris Fernandez to lead its workplace management business across the Americas, where he will oversee teams, enhance operational performance and deploy future capabilities to accelerate growth. In his new role, Fernandez will leverage his two decades of corporate real estate experience to drive strategy for workplace optimization, facilities management, engineering, employee experience, technical building services, sustainable operations and occupancy management. Josh Koons has been hired as vice president of facilities for PsiQuantum in Chicago, helping lead
bullets: Cresa hires Julie Northcutt-Dunn as President, Markets from Colliers (Chicago HQ); covers 6-city Midwest region.
Inland Real Estate adds capital formation head (Parikh, 20 yrs CRE investing) and general counsel to institutional platform.
JLL, PREMIER Design, and other CRE firms expand leadership in workplace and construction services across U.S.
stories.summary: Midwest commercial real estate firms are scaling institutional and capital-raising infrastructure. Cresa relocated a Colliers exec overseeing Chicago, Indianapolis, Kansas City, Omaha, St. Louis, and Nashville to drive organic growth. Inland Real Estate hired experienced institutional capital and legal talent. JLL, PREMIER, and Marcus & Millichap are all promoting national and regional leadership, signaling confidence in deal flow and client growth.
stories.impact: Personnel moves reflect consolidation of advisory and capital-raising power among large firms. For multifamily developers, this means brokerage and advisory services are concentrating among fewer platforms with deeper institutional relationships; smaller independents may struggle to access same-tier capital introductions and advisory depth. Chicago remains a capital hub for Midwest multifamily strategies.

stories.title: Report: Has Brexit Been a Boon to the UK?
stories.excerpt: On June 23, 2016, citizens turned out in a national referendum to decide whether the United Kingdom should withdraw from the European Union. After the dust settled, 51.89% voted in favor of departing, while 48.11% voted to “Remain.” By December 2020, the UK officially left the EU. Ten years after the highly contentious vote that became known as Brexit, the question is whether the UK has benefited from it. According to economist Ray Perryman, not so much. In his recently released column, “Brexit—10 Years Later,” the president and CEO of The Perryman Group said that “the data clearly indicates that Brexit has indeed severely harmed the UK economy.” Why Leave? Many reasons led to the vote to “Leave.” Perryman said key issues included a desire for greater control and reduced immigration. Additionally, “proponents thought the UK would not only save billions of pounds in EU membership fees but would also be able to negotiate better trade deals without the EU bureaucracy,” he said. Other reasons included a massive publicity campaign by big-gun proponents, including Boris Johnson (former mayor of London) and Michael Gove (justice secretary), distrust of Prime Minister David Cameron (a proponent of the Remain campaign), and Labor’s failure to connect with voters. Adding to the issue were older voters who flocked to the polls, with research noting that Brexit support was “significantly higher among those aged 55 and over.” Meanwhile, in 2026 . . . Statista said that as of June 2026, 57% of UK citizens surveyed in a YouGov poll said their country shouldn’t have left the EU. Additionally, a July 2025 YouGov survey reported that “most people in the UK thought that Brexit had a mainly negative impact, especially on the cost of living and the economy,” Statista said. The opinion is based on fact. Quoting a study by the Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research, Perryman said that Brexit had reduced the UK’s Gross Domestic Product by 6% to 8%, while investment has fallen by 1
bullets: Brexit (10 years post-referendum): UK economy severely harmed per economist Ray Perryman; 57% of citizens regret vote.
Immigration control and cost-of-living concerns drove Leave vote; 2026 polling shows public sentiment reversed.
No direct UK economic recovery; trade friction and reduced business dynamism cited as primary harms.
stories.summary: Ten years after the 2016 Brexit referendum, UK economic data show severe contraction and continued hardship. Economist Ray Perryman's analysis concludes the economy has been 'severely harmed.' As of June 2026, 57% of UK citizens surveyed say the country should not have left the EU, and most blame Brexit for negative impacts on cost of living and growth. Initial promises of fiscal savings and better trade deals have not materialized.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to U.S. multifamily development. UK regulatory and economic uncertainty may affect some international capital flows, but U.S. apartment markets are not materially dependent on UK capital or immigration. Could be relevant only if analyzing cross-border capital redeployment or considering UK-based investor portfolio rebalancing toward U.S. assets—a minor secondary effect.

stories.title: New York People and Company News, Week of June 26, 2026
stories.excerpt: Newmark Group, Inc. has hired Munish Viralam as Executive Vice Chairman to lead its Real Estate Strategy & Consulting Group. Based in New York, Viralam leads the group that combines Newmark’s New York Consulting and Financial Services teams, including Jason Perla and Romel Cañete. The practice serves as a centralized resource supporting Newmark’s brokerage teams, and complements Newmark’s broader real estate advisory capabilities. Allen Matkins announced that Ronald R. Rossi has joined the firm’s Litigation Practice as a partner in its New York office. A first-chair trial lawyer and lead counsel in complex, high-stakes commercial disputes with more than three decades of experience, Rossi is often called up to represent clients in their most significant business, real estate, insurance, technology, labor, and corporate governance disputes in courts and arbitration forums across the United States and internationally. Eastern Union, one of the nation’s leading commercial real estate mortgage brokerages, announced the next phase of its nationwide expansion, led by Chairman Ira Zlotowitz and President Abe Bergman, and the signing of top-producing broker Elliott Kunstlinger as the first senior broker to join under the plan. Kunstlinger, known for closing some of the market’s largest and most complex financings, is the first of several senior and emerging brokers now in discussions to join the firm. Colliers has appointed industry veteran Giles Wrench as EVP, Head of Enterprise Client Solutions, U.S. | Global Occupier Services, where he will lead the firm’s enterprise client growth strategy and help expand technology-enabled real estate solutions for major corporate occupiers. Wrench joins from JLL, where he helped build solutions for Fortune 500 clients and most recently launched a financial services industry vertical. A&G Real Estate Partners, a leading real estate advisory firm specializing in sales of real estate and lease re
bullets: Newmark hires Munish Viralam as EVX to lead Real Estate Strategy & Consulting Group in New York.
Eastern Union announces nationwide expansion; signs top broker Elliott Kunstlinger, known for large complex financings.
Colliers, JLL promote executives in enterprise solutions and workplace strategy roles across U.S.
stories.summary: New York–based commercial real estate advisory and brokerage firms are consolidating senior talent and expanding national platforms. Newmark created a centralized Real Estate Strategy & Consulting Group to support brokerage teams. Eastern Union is aggressively recruiting senior brokers to lead national expansion. Colliers and JLL are promoting veteran advisors into enterprise and workplace solutions roles.
stories.impact: Similar to article 3: advisory and brokerage power is concentrating in large, nationally branded firms. For multifamily developers seeking financing or institutional capital, relationship and platform depth now favor large brokerages; smaller or regional firms face talent drain. Debt and equity sourcing will increasingly flow through these consolidated advisory platforms, raising bar for deal access.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Names National Lead for Net Lease Division
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap said Thursday that Thomas Gorman has been appointed senior managing director and leader of its national net lease division. In the newly created role, Gorman will oversee the ongoing growth of the national net lease division, partner with agents to strengthen existing industry relationships, and untap strategic growth opportunities. “Thomas is a proven leader and accomplished commercial real estate executive whose experience in net leased retail investment sales, institutional advisory, and brokerage operations make him the ideal leader for our expanding national net lease division,” said Gregory A. LaBerge, Marcus & Millichap’s chief client officer. During a career spanning more than 20 years, Gorman has held senior positions with national commercial real estate platforms, advising institutional investors, REITs, developers, and private clients on net lease acquisitions, dispositions, financing and portfolio strategy. He began his career in commercial real estate as a single-tenant net-leased investment advisor. Along with being a successful broker, Gorman has recruited, trained and mentored investment sales teams and launched brokerage offices. The post Marcus & Millichap Names National Lead for Net Lease Division appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap appoints Thomas Gorman as Senior Managing Director, national net lease division (new role).
Gorman: 20+ years in net-lease retail, institutional advisory, and brokerage operations; proven recruiter and leader.
Firm signaling priority expansion in 1031 exchange and NNN retail investment sales to institutional and REIT buyers.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap created a dedicated national net lease division leadership role and hired Thomas Gorman, a veteran broker and advisor with two decades of experience in NNN retail acquisitions, dispositions, financing, and portfolio strategy for institutions, REITs, and developers. Gorman will oversee division growth, broker partnerships, and institutional relationships.
stories.impact: Indicates continued capital availability and investor demand for credit-rated, NNN retail assets (1031 flows, institutions, REITs), but reinforces that this capital is flowing to retail, not multifamily. For multifamily developers, this signals net-lease and other debt-free retail asset classes are still attractive to institutional allocators; multifamily demand faces relative headwinds in capital competition.

stories.title: Houston Rodeo partially breaks from NRG Park with $300M livestock campus
stories.excerpt: The Houston Livestock Show and Rodeo is partially bucking NRG Park to build its own $300 million facility about five miles east.  The Rodeo is building a 1 million-square-foot complex at the corner of Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. Part of the project will be the construction of their own arena, but some operations will remain at NRG Park. The Rodeo is working on a new lease with the Houston Texans as well as Harris County, according to the Houston Business Journal.  Houston Rodeo president and CEO Chris Boleman told the outlet that this is not a relocation, even though […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston Livestock Show & Rodeo breaks from NRG Park; building $300M, 1M-SF campus at Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard.
New complex (860K SF barns, 140K SF barn, auction hall, outdoor yards) opens year-round events; main rodeo stays at NRG.
Clay Development & Construction (GC), Populous (design); project part of rodeo's 100-year vision and 2032 centennial.
stories.summary: The Houston Livestock Show & Rodeo is committing $300 million to build a dedicated 1 million-square-foot livestock and event campus about five miles east of NRG Park at Highway 288 and Airport Boulevard. The facility will include two large barns (860K SF), a central barn (140K SF), auction hall, and outdoor cattle yards, enabling year-round agricultural events while marquee rodeo shows remain at NRG. Construction begins fall 2026; Trammell Crow Company (developer), Populous (architect), Clay Development (GC).
stories.impact: Signals major institutional amenity/event infrastructure investment in outer Houston corridor (Highway 288), indicating confidence in suburban accessibility and long-term event-driven tourism demand. For multifamily developers, this suggests a secondary growth vector along Highway 288 corridor with complementary event/hospitality employment; opportunity to acquire/develop workforce and visitor-serving apartments near new rodeo campus in coming years, ahead of 2032 centennial activation.

stories.title: Developer looks to bring almost 300 units to Flushing
stories.excerpt: Flushing is poised to receive an influx of nearly 300 units — many affordable — should Gary Chen secure a zoning change. On Monday, Chen filed an application with the New York City Department of City Planning for a mixed-use project at 32-02 Linden Place in the Queens neighborhood, the Commercial Observer reported. The zoning change would be bound by 32nd Avenue, Linden Place and Farrington Street. Chen wants to build a 10-story, 296-unit complex at the site, which would also include 33,000 square feet of commercial space and nearly 200 parking spaces. Roughly 25 percent of the units would […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gary Chen files zoning change for 296-unit, 10-story mixed-use at 32-02 Linden Place, Flushing, Queens.
25% affordable units (74 units), 33K SF retail, 200 parking spaces; assemblage purchased 2024 for $26.5M.
Chen owns ~12 other Queens properties; developer competing for zoning approvals alongside Grun Group (108 units Kew Gardens) and others.
stories.summary: Developer Gary Chen submitted a zoning application to NYC Department of City Planning for a 296-unit, 10-story mixed-use residential project at 32-02 Linden Place in Flushing, Queens. The project would include 33,000 square feet of retail and roughly 200 parking spaces; 25% of units (approximately 74) designated as affordable. Chen acquired the four-parcel assemblage in 2024 for $26.5 million. The proposal aligns with NYC's transit-accessible housing goals.
stories.impact: Queens (Flushing submarket) is seeing multiple concurrent zoning applications for moderate-scale multifamily (200–300 units), signaling developer confidence in transit-accessible upzoning and affordable housing incentives. For multifamily operators and acquirers, Flushing zoning liberalization could yield new rental supply (if approved) or value-add conversions; monitor approval pace and competitive development density to assess rental rate pressure and hold-period returns.

stories.title: Habitat for Humanity to Build 179 Co-op Units in Brooklyn and the Bronx
stories.excerpt: Habitat for Humanity is building out an ecosystem in the outer boroughs, Commercial Observer has learned. The not-for-profit homebuilder, known for building houses in disaster- and poverty-stricken communities, has announced it will construct a 108-unit co-operative development spanning eight sites across Brooklyn, as well as an additional building in the Bronx. The buildings utilize a number of city, state and federal programs to subsidize the projects, such as the Open Door Program and the New Infill Housing Opportunity Program, both provided by the New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) and the state’s Homes and Community Renewal program. The Constellation development in Brooklyn started as a 77-unit development before Habitat for Humanity grew the planned community to 108 units. Meanwhile the Bronx c-op, known as Claremont House, was originally 40 units before its expansion to 71 units. The two developments represent Habitat for Humanity’s largest construction pipeline in its history, and Claremont House will be the largest development it has ever built, according to Sabrina Lippman, CEO of the organization in New York City and Westchester County. Both expansion plans were made possible by the City of Yes for Housing Opportunity, the pro-development reforms instituted under former Mayor Eric Adams. “We serve working New Yorkers, so nurses, teachers, civil service workers, all the folks that are the backbone of the city who just do not have access to having a stake in the city they help run,” Lippman told CO. “I wish there were more of us building affordable homeownership buildings, but given how difficult these capital stacks are to put together, it’s hard to keep up with the demand that the city deserves.” Lippman added that this type of housing targets not the poorest residents, but also not the wealthiest, instead addressing a “missing middle” for whom homeownership would not be possible. “When people think Habitat, they think
bullets: Habitat for Humanity building 108 co-op units across 8 Brooklyn sites and 71-unit Claremont House in Bronx.
Projects leverage NYC HPD (Open Door, Infill Housing), NYS Homes & Community Renewal programs; both grew from smaller original plans.
Co-op model and subsidy stacking enable affordability without market-rate cross-subsidy or developer profit.
stories.summary: Habitat for Humanity NYC announced a 179-unit co-op development: 108 units across eight Brooklyn sites (Constellation) and 71 units in the Bronx (Claremont House). Both projects expanded from smaller initial plans and leverage NYC HPD and state affordable housing programs (Open Door Program, Infill Housing, Homes & Community Renewal) to subsidize construction and operations. Ownership structure is limited-equity cooperative, not rental.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to for-profit multifamily operators. Highlights that subsidy-stacked co-op developments (not rental) are a material supply vector in outer boroughs; for rental multifamily, this represents permanent removal of land/sites from rental-supply development and competes for public subsidy attention. Developers should note that city and state capital increasingly favors ownership/co-op models in outer boroughs, potentially raising cost of debt and equity for rental projects.

stories.title: Al Adelson of the Berkeley Palm Beach: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: Developer Al Adelson helped usher in West Palm Beach’s new era of high-end condo developments with the Bristol, a pre-pandemic building that proved buyers would pay $1,000 a square foot for units west of the Intracoastal Waterway. Now Adelson is developing The Berkeley Palm Beach, a condo tower at 500 South Australian Avenue, on the western edge of Downtown West Palm Beach. With units priced at $2 million to $10 million, Adelson again is pushing the pricing envelope. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: I remember when you came to market with very aggressive pricing for the Bristol, and I was skeptical that it would sell. How did you know that price point was possible? Al Adelson: I have been in Florida for 25 years. I observed the market very closely, and I did studies. I interviewed almost 100 different brokers from Palm Beach to West Palm Beach, and I got a very, very good understanding. I did a lot of research on the island, and I understand very well how those people who lived there – I lived there myself for a few years – think and feel. I realized that there was a hardcore 20 percent of people who reside on Palm Beach Island who wouldn’t move off of it if you gave them something for nothing. You know, just dedicated to the island. Not only are they not bothered by the rules and regulations, they’re in favor of them — such as they have no high-rises allowed to be built above six stories. They have thousands of units that are very old, and it’s almost impossible to tear something down and build new, because the cost of the land is so prohibitive. And there’s no chance to go high, so you don’t get to spread out your cost over a large number of units. So, therefore, I felt very strongly that West Palm would have an opportunity to pick up a whole bunch of units, and I put my money where my mouth was. I did the Bristol, and it became beyond phenomenally successful. I think it’s
bullets: Al Adelson developing The Berkeley Palm Beach condo tower; units priced $2M–$10M at 500 South Australian Avenue, West Palm Beach.
Adelson's prior Bristol project proved $1K/SF pricing viable west of Intracoastal; new tower pushes pricing further.
Market research (100 broker interviews, behavioral analysis) informed aggressive pricing strategy and target buyer profile.
stories.summary: Developer Al Adelson is building The Berkeley Palm Beach, a luxury condo tower at 500 South Australian Avenue on the western edge of Downtown West Palm Beach, with units priced $2 million to $10 million. Adelson's prior success with the Bristol—which sold at $1,000/SF west of the Intracoastal—informed confidence in aggressive pricing for this project. Adelson conducted extensive market research (interviews with 100+ brokers, behavioral analysis of existing residents) to validate demand.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily rental operators. Signals strong ultra-high-net-worth demand in South Florida (West Palm Beach, Miami metros) for ownership-model luxury condos; rental multifamily in these markets competes with condo conversion and condo rental arbitrage. For multifamily developers, rising condo pricing may pressure rental yields and tenant acquisition in high-end South Florida segments; consider mixed-use or condo-first strategies if targeting affluent buyer/renter demographics.

stories.title: Dallas secures $19M worth of incentives for potential Morgan Stanley move
stories.excerpt: Dallas City Council greenlit an $18.5 million incentive package Wednesday for Morgan Stanley’s potential Y’all Street move.  The financial services and investment bank will spend a little time in Downtown Dallas before moving to Uptown in 2031. The package coincides with Morgan Stanley earmarking $700 million for its new digs at 2401 McKinney Avenue, which will bring hundreds of employees to Dallas’s burgeoning financial hub.  Morgan Stanley has yet to formally communicate their plans, but documents filed with the city indicate the timeline, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Morgan Stanley can still opt out and head to Alpharetta, Georgia, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dallas City Council approves $18.5M incentives for Morgan Stanley HQ move; bank commits $700M, Trammell Crow $650M total development cost $1.3B.
700K SF Uptown office campus at 2401 McKinney Avenue targets 3,800 employees; construction begins fall 2026, opens 2031.
Temporary 255K SF Downtown space (Fountain Place) during 5-year construction; Morgan Stanley can still opt out for Alpharetta, GA.
stories.summary: Dallas City Council approved an $18.5 million incentive package for Morgan Stanley to relocate its headquarters to Uptown. The bank is committing $700 million for a new 700,000-square-foot campus at 2401 McKinney Avenue, targeting approximately 3,800 employees. Trammell Crow Company is developing the project with $650 million investment; total development cost approximately $1.3 billion. Construction begins fall 2026, with occupancy in 2031. Morgan Stanley will temporarily occupy 255,000 SF at Fountain Place Downtown during construction.
stories.impact: Major labor demand inflection in Dallas Uptown submarket: 3,800 direct Morgan Stanley jobs plus secondary professional-services employment (law, accounting, consulting) drives sustained rental demand for workforce housing in Uptown and nearby urban core through 2031–2035. Multifamily developers should prioritize Uptown/McKinney corridor acquisitions and new development; rents likely to sustain 3–5% annual growth. Secondary impact: temporary Downtown activation may stabilize struggling CBD office market but unlikely to drive multifamily demand there; focus remains Uptown.

stories.title: Skyscrapers encouraged: Development near transit in California’s biggest cities poised for major boost from new bill
stories.excerpt: Transit-oriented development in major cities across the Golden State is poised to take a much firmer shape with a piece of legislation that could help municipalities close the gap between housing demand and state-mandated planning goals. Assembly Bill 2074, dubbed the Downtown Revitalization Act, is quickly working its way through the state legislature. The California State Assembly passed the proposed law in a 55-5 vote on May 28 and the State Senate amended and referred it to the Senate Housing Committee last week. Authored by Assembly member Matt Haney of San Francisco, AB 2074 would supercharge housing development in seven […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: California AB 2074 (Downtown Revitalization Act) mandates transit-oriented housing hubs in 7 major cities; minimum 150 ft, 450 ft+ buildings allowed.
State-backed $500M low-interest loan fund for developers; expedited permitting for projects meeting labor standards.
Targets: LA, San Diego, San Jose, San Francisco, Sacramento, Oakland, Long Beach; legislation advancing (Assembly passed 55–5, Senate reviewing).
stories.summary: California Assembly Bill 2074 (Downtown Revitalization Act) would mandate creation of high-density housing districts in seven major cities—Los Angeles, San Diego, San Jose, San Francisco, Sacramento, Oakland, and Long Beach—with minimum building heights of 150 feet and allowing up to 450+ feet. The bill includes a $500 million state-backed revolving loan fund to lower construction costs for developers and streamlines permitting for projects meeting labor standards. The Assembly passed it 55–5 on May 28; Senate is reviewing.
stories.impact: If enacted, AB 2074 creates major entitlement and financing tailwind for transit-rich TOD multifamily in California's largest metros. $500M loan fund reduces cost of capital; streamlined approvals shrink risk timeline. For Trammell Crow and other West Coast developers, this significantly de-risks LA, San Francisco, San Jose, Oakland feasibility and front-loads site control in designated TOD zones. Monitor Senate passage; if approved, expect rapid permit applications and construction starts in Q4 2026–Q1 2027.

stories.title: S3 Capital Lends $101M on Luxury Resort Project Near Orlando
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Urban Network Capital Group and Vertical Developments has landed $101 million of construction financing to build a master-planned luxury resort community in suburban Orlando, Commercial Observer has learned. S3 Capital provided the loan for the sponsorship’s multiphased Visions Orlando Resort & Spa project in Davenport, Fla. The 70-acre project when completed will comprise a mix of single-family homes, townhomes and condos along with resident amenities and retail. “Visions Orlando represents exactly the type of thoughtfully designed residential community we look to support,” Steven Jemal, managing director of origination at S3 Capital, said in a statement. “From a lender’s perspective, the presale-driven capitalization provides significant downside protection while the underlying tourism dynamics support long-term liquidity and buyer demand.” Located at 7007 Osceola Polk Line Road, the Visions Orlando Resort development has sold out all 460 residential units in Phases I, II, III and V, according to S3. Phase IV of the project features 277 condo-hotel units geared toward vacationers. The master-planned community is expected to expand through Phase VIII along with a 4-acre commercial component, according to S3. Community amenities will include a 10,000-square-foot clubhouse with a fitness center, pool, sports courts, wellness facilities, restaurant, bar, movie theater and golf simulator. Urban Network Capital Group and Vertical Developments did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached t acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: S3 Capital provides $101M construction financing for Visions Orlando Resort & Spa, 70-acre master-planned community in Davenport (suburban Orlando).
Developer JV (Urban Network Capital, Vertical Developments); 460 units (single-family, townhomes, condos) sold out in Phase 1.
Financing underscores presale-driven capitalization and tourism-adjacent demand in Orlando metro exurban ring.
stories.summary: S3 Capital financed a $101 million construction loan for Visions Orlando Resort & Spa, a master-planned, 70-acre residential and mixed-use community in Davenport, Florida (suburban Orlando). The joint venture (Urban Network Capital & Vertical Developments) sold out all 460 units in Phase 1, comprising single-family homes, townhomes, and condos plus retail and amenities. Lender highlighted presale strength and tourism-adjacent location as downside protection.
stories.impact: Signals strong presale momentum and construction financing availability for Orlando metro exurban resort-adjacent mixed-use residential. Presale-driven deals offer lenders comfort; multifamily developers should consider hybrid resort/residential models in secondary Florida metros (Davenport, Kissimmee) where tourism jobs, airport access, and lifestyle amenities support rental demand. Orlando metro rents remain pressure-tested; resort-integrated communities may justify premium rents.

stories.title: Law Firm Alston & Bird Takes 170K SF at 51 West 52nd Street
stories.excerpt: International law firm Alston & Bird has signed a major deal to relocate its offices from Park Avenue. Alston & Bird, which provides legal counsel to Fortune 100 companies and private institutions, signed a 15-year, 169,664-square-foot lease at Harbor Group International’s 51 West 52nd Street, with plans to move its operations from 90 Park Avenue, according to landlord broker Newmark. Newmark did not disclose the asking rent for the building, also known as 51W52, but the average office asking rent in Midtown was $86.55 per square foot in May, according to a report from CBRE. “51W52 is now fully leased to a roster of prominent tenants, all of whom match the building’s prestige,” Jordan Slone, chairman and CEO of Harbor Group International, said in a statement. “The success of 51W52 validates our strong faith in the resilience of the New York office market, especially for the best trophy properties in the best locations.” Newmark’s Howard Hersch, Greg Conen, Scott Klau, Erik Harris, Jennifer Schreiber and Zach Weil negotiated on behalf of the landlord, while Brad Wolk, Matthew Barlow, David Goldstein, Joe Learner, Matthew Brainard and Laura Whelan of Savills represented the tenant. Savills did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Recent deals in the ominous 38-story tower include Kroll Bond Rating Agency taking 121,000 square feet in January and law firm Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe renewing its 144,312-square-foot lease in July 2024. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Alston & Bird signs 15-year, 170K-SF lease at 51 West 52nd Street, Manhattan; relocates from Park Avenue.
Harbor Group International (HGI) landlord; building now fully leased; asking rent ~$86.55/SF (Midtown avg May 2026).
Law firm anchor confirms trophy office recovery and sustained demand from white-collar tenants for best-location Midtown assets.
stories.summary: International law firm Alston & Bird signed a 15-year lease for 169,664 square feet at Harbor Group International's 51 West 52nd Street in Midtown Manhattan, relocating from 90 Park Avenue. The lease brings the building to full occupancy; estimated asking rent approximately $86.55 per square foot (Midtown average). Landlord CEO Jordan Slone stated the deal validates resilience of premium New York office market.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Signals trophy office properties in Manhattan are stabilizing and leasing to high-credit tenants, but this does NOT indicate broad office recovery or job growth; rather, it reflects flight-to-quality and consolidation into best-location assets. For NYC multifamily developers, strong Manhattan office leasing does not guarantee residential demand—CBD office tenants are older, lower-density, lower-household-growth demographic. Focus remains on transit-accessible neighborhoods (Queens, Brooklyn) with younger, growing workforce.

stories.title: Charney, Tavros snag $785M financing for historic Gowanus project
stories.excerpt: A joint venture led by Charney Companies and Tavros secured a new package of debt and equity for their historic Gowanus development. Charney, Tavros and Incoco Capital landed $785 million in financing for 175 Third Street, part of the larger Gowanus Wharf campus in Brooklyn, the Commercial Observer reported. It’s the fifth building in the broader vision for the neighborhood. Apollo and Affinius Capital issued $600 million in debt for the project, representing the fourth development in Gowanus Wharf involving the latter party. The former has financed development in the neighborhood previously. Additionally, Scott Rechler’s RXR injected $185 million in […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Charney, Tavros, Incoco land $785M debt and equity for 175 Third Street, Gowanus Wharf, Brooklyn (5th phase).
Apollo ($600M debt), Affinius ($600M debt involvement, 4th Gowanus project), RXR ($185M equity); JLL Capital Markets brokered.
1.1M SF, 27 stories, 1,100 units; RFR remediated/designed; 421-a tax abatement in place; presale and lender confidence strong.
stories.summary: Charney Companies, Tavros, and Incoco secured $785 million in financing for 175 Third Street in the Gowanus Wharf campus: $600 million senior debt (Apollo & Affinius) and $185 million equity (RXR). The project is the fifth phase of the broader Gowanus Wharf redevelopment and will deliver 1,100 units across 27 stories and 1.1 million square feet. Environmental remediation and design were completed by prior owner RFR; project benefits from a 421-a tax abatement. Strong lender appetite reflects success of earlier Gowanus phases.
stories.impact: Gowanus (Brooklyn) multiphase waterfront redevelopment demonstrates sustained institutional capital appetite (Apollo, Affinius, RXR) for large-scale, long-hold multifamily in Brooklyn with tax incentive support. Financing quantum ($785M for 1.1M SF, ~$714/SF) reflects competitive cap rates and strong investor conviction. For Trammell Crow and others, Gowanus validates Brooklyn waterfront as capital-accessible submarket; however, deal success hinges on 421-a tax abatement and presale momentum—regulatory risk remains if abatement program changes.

stories.title: Is DC the next hot luxury resi space? BSH panelists talk about navigating capital’s emerging high-end market
stories.excerpt: Long defined by easily-turned-over efficiency apartments, DC builders are working overtime to meet new demand for luxury housing in the nation’s capital. Last week, BSH and TRD brought representatives of the city’s design, development and operations communities together for a frank discussion about what’s working, and what isn’t, in the DC luxury space. The event was hosted at the BSH Experience & Design Center, the capital’s home for everything Bosch, Thermador and Gaggenau. Across the three speakers’ portfolios, one trend has emerged: today’s luxury buyers don’t crave fancy pools or double height lobbies. For them, convenient amenities, services and retailers […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: DC luxury multifamily demand rising; builders shifting from amenity-heavy (pools, lobbies) to time-saving services and local retail.
EYA, District Wharf Properties leading; co-working, private services, authentic neighborhood programming drive retention.
Luxury buyer preference for lifestyle integration and convenience over trophy amenities reshaping development and operating strategy.
stories.summary: Washington, DC has emerged as a growing luxury residential market, and developers and operators are learning that high-end renters prioritize time-saving services and local retail integration over traditional luxury amenities (pools, double-height lobbies, fitness). EYA and District Wharf Properties are redesigning entire communities around co-working spaces, programming, and retail environments that serve daily lifestyle needs. District Wharf Properties CEO Andrew Son emphasized creating 'a sense of place' with programming before residents move in.
stories.impact: DC luxury multifamily is entering maturation phase with refined underwriting: developers should prioritize retail/F&B partnerships, service-oriented amenities (co-working, concierge, dry cleaning integration), and neighborhood activation (free events, programming) over capital-intensive building-systems amenities. For Trammell Crow and other DC operators, this signals tenant preference for mixed-use, walkable, programmed environments; standalone buildings without surrounding activation face tenant acquisition and retention headwinds. Consider ground-floor retail partnerships and placemaking investment critical to DC luxury rents.
